risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) of the project using a personal computer before subject recruitment." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence, which is an explicit random method and is sufficient for Low risk of bias for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Eligible selected subjects signed the consent form and completed the baseline measures ... before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group. The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) ... before subject recruitment." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelope mechanism opened only after consent and baseline assessment, which prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blinded randomized controlled trial (RCT)" and "before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group." Comment: The trial was only single-blinded, and the description indicates the counselor learned allocation when the envelope was opened. Participants also received different amounts of free NRT (1 week vs 2 weeks), so participants and personnel delivering the intervention were plausibly aware of assignment. Because the main outcomes were smoking cessation and related self-reported abstinence measures, knowledge of allocation could reasonably influence behavior, counseling delivery, and reporting, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "An independent interviewer, who was unaware of the subject's group allocation, carried out the 6 and 12 months follow-up interview." Comment: The main outcomes were largely self-reported smoking abstinence, which was assessed by an independent interviewer blinded to allocation; for the biochemically validated outcome, expired carbon monoxide was measured among those who attended for validation, an objective measure unlikely to be influenced by lack of blinding. This supports low risk of detection bias for outcome assessment.

Quote: "We conducted all analyses on an intention-to-treat basis. Follow-up variables with missing data were set to their baseline values (e.g. current smoker)." Comment: The paper clearly reports ITT analysis with a prespecified method for handling missing follow-up data, and the sample sizes for outcomes remain the full randomized denominators (A1 n=284; A2 n=278) at 6 and 12 months, indicating attrition was handled conservatively and does not appear likely to have biased the result. Although the paper notes some loss to follow-up, it does not suggest marked imbalance or outcome-related dropout, and overall follow-up appears adequate for a low-risk judgement.
